original_hypothesis.title: Retroactive Reward Rate Modification via setRewards for Unconsumed Past Epochs
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: setRewards() can modify cantoPerBlock for any epoch, including past epochs that have not yet been processed by update_market for all markets. Since update_market is lazily evaluated (only runs when explicitly called), governance calling setRewards for a past epoch changes the reward rate that will be retroactively applied when a stale market's update_market finally runs. This is a missing validation — there is no check that `_fromEpoch >= current_epoch`.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Even non-malicious governance could accidentally overwrite past epoch rates during a routine rate-setting operation. The code should enforce that only future/current epochs can be modified. Since different markets may be at different lastRewardBlock positions, modifying a past epoch affects markets inconsistently — some may have already consumed that epoch's rate while others haven't, creating unfair reward distribution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setRewards validates epoch alignment but NOT that epochs are current or future; cantoPerBlock[past_epoch] can be overwritten

preconditions: Governance calls setRewards with _fromEpoch pointing to an already-elapsed epoch
At least one market has lastRewardBlock before the modified epoch (hasn't consumed it yet)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: cantoPerBlock[past_epoch] overwritten after epoch has elapsed
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Governance (mistakenly or maliciously) changes past rates; stale markets retroactively get different rewards
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Markets that haven't been updated receive inflated or deflated rewards compared to markets that were already updated with the original rate

economic_questions: How many markets are typically stale enough to be affected by a past-epoch change?
What is the maximum reward differential from a single retroactive rate change?
Can governance accidentally set past epochs through a range that includes both past and future?
validation_requirements: Test setRewards for a past epoch and verify it changes cantoPerBlock
Call update_market on a stale market after the change and verify altered rewards
Compare rewards between a market that processed the epoch before vs after the change

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to entire epoch's reward allocation for affected markets
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on rate differential and stale market balances
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction to modify; effect realized on next update_market call

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When multiple markets are stale and haven't consumed recent epochs

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users in markets that haven't been updated recently
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to retroactive rate change magnitude
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All rewards for affected epochs across stale markets

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical governance setRewards call patterns, market staleness
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify no time-guard exists on setRewards epoch range

validation_result.impact_summary: setRewards() allows governance to retroactively modify cantoPerBlock for past epochs. Markets with stale lastRewardBlock will use the modified rate when update_market finally runs, creating inconsistent reward distribution compared to markets that already consumed the original rate.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The setRewards function at LendingLedger.sol lines 107-113 only validates epoch alignment (% BLOCK_EPOCH == 0) but performs no temporal check to prevent modification of past epochs. Since update_market is lazily evaluated (only runs when explicitly called via sync_ledger or claim), different markets may have consumed different epochs. When governance modifies cantoPerBlock for a past epoch, markets that already processed that epoch used the old rate, while markets that haven't processed it yet will use the new rate. This is a missing validation defect that enables retroactive, inconsistent reward distribution.

The defect exists independently of whether governance acts maliciously or accidentally. A routine governance call to set rewards for a range that includes both past and future epochs would trigger this bug.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 107-113 of LendingLedger.sol clearly show the setRewards function with only an epoch alignment check. Lines 63-65 of update_market show that cantoPerBlock[epoch] is read at call time, not at the time the epoch actually occurred. The combination means any modification to cantoPerBlock for a past epoch will retroactively affect markets that haven't yet been updated through that epoch.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No check that _fromEpoch >= (block.number / BLOCK_EPOCH) * BLOCK_EPOCH. Past epochs can be freely overwritten, affecting stale markets that haven't consumed those epochs yet.

cantoPerBlock[epoch] is read at the time of the call, not snapshotted. If governance modified the value for a past epoch after some markets already processed it, this function will use the new value for markets that haven't processed it, creating inconsistency.

calling_sequence: 4. Market A already had update_market called through epoch 200000 — used old rate
5. Market B has lastRewardBlock < 200000 — hasn't processed epoch 200000 yet
6. When update_market(marketB) is called, line 63-65 reads cantoPerBlock[200000] which is now newRate
7. Market B gets rewards calculated with newRate while Market A used oldRate for the same epoch
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path starts at setRewards (line 101) where governance provides a _fromEpoch that has already elapsed. The only validation at line 107 checks epoch alignment (% BLOCK_EPOCH == 0) but not temporal validity. The loop at line 108-110 overwrites cantoPerBlock for all epochs in range. Later, when update_market is called for a stale market (line 55), the while loop at line 61 iterates from lastRewardBlock. At line 63, epoch is computed, and at line 65, cantoPerBlock[epoch] is read — now returning the modified value. This means the stale market processes the past epoch with the new rate, while markets already updated used the original rate.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The defect is inherent in the contract code regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: Epoch 200,000 has cantoPerBlock = 1e18. Market A is actively used and update_market was called at block 250,000 (consumed epoch 200,000 with rate 1e18). Market B has lastRewardBlock = 150,000 (stale). At block 300,000, governance calls setRewards(200000, 300000, 2e18) intending to double rewards going forward but accidentally including the past epoch. cantoPerBlock[200000] is now 2e18. When someone calls update_market(marketB), the loop processes epoch 200,000 with rate 2e18 instead of the original 1e18. Market B users receive double the rewards for epoch 200,000 compared to Market A users. This is unfair and represents a code defect — the missing validation allows unintended retroactive changes.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The impact is inconsistent reward distribution across markets for the same epoch. Markets that were recently updated use the original rate, while stale markets use the modified rate. This can result in over-distribution (inflating rewards beyond what was budgeted) or under-distribution (reducing earned rewards). The severity depends on the rate differential and the number of stale markets. Even without malicious intent, a routine governance operation that sets rewards for a range spanning past and future epochs would trigger this bug.
recommended_mitigations: Add a temporal check in setRewards: require(_fromEpoch >= (block.number / BLOCK_EPOCH) * BLOCK_EPOCH, 'Cannot modify past epochs')
Alternatively, require _fromEpoch > block.number to only allow setting future epochs
Consider snapshotting cantoPerBlock values at epoch start so they cannot be retroactively modified
Add an event emission for setRewards to enable off-chain monitoring of rate changes
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LendingLedger with a mock GaugeController. 2. Whitelist two markets A and B at the same block. 3. Deposit into both markets via sync_ledger. 4. Set cantoPerBlock for epoch E to rate R1 via setRewards. 5. Advance blocks past epoch E. 6. Call update_market(A) to consume epoch E with rate R1. 7. Call setRewards to change epoch E's rate to R2. 8. Call update_market(B) — verify it uses R2 for epoch E. 9. Compare accCantoPerShare between A and B — they should differ, confirming the bug.
uncertainties: The practical impact depends on how frequently governance calls setRewards and whether past epochs are included in the range
The severity also depends on how stale markets typically are in production
additional_testing_needed: Foundry test that verifies cantoPerBlock for a past epoch can be overwritten
Test comparing accCantoPerShare across two markets after retroactive rate change
Fuzz test with random epoch ranges in setRewards to identify boundary conditions
